methods.description: Procedures for collecting various types of water samples (rainfall, throughfall, stream water, well water, soil water, overland flow) from the study catchment and performing initial processing steps like splitting, storage, and filtration.

steps.description: Fortnightly, cumulative rainfall (R) and throughfall samples under deciduous trees (TH1) and coniferous trees (TH2) were collected using conical, volumetric rain gauges.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect cumulative rainfall and throughfall samples using rain gauges on a fortnightly basis.

steps.description: A tenbottle sequential rainfall sampler was installed at the rain gauge located within the Weierbach (modified from Kennedy et al., 1979).
steps.searchable_summary: Install and operate a sequential rainfall sampler for event-based collection at a specific rain gauge location.

steps.description: Three automatic water samplers (ISCO 3700 FS and 6712 FS) were installed immediately upstream of the weir to collect stream water samples (AS) frequently (0.5 to 4 h) during storm events.Sampling was triggered by flow conditions.
steps.searchable_summary: Install and operate automatic water samplers for frequent stream water collection during storm events, triggered by flow conditions.
outputs: Frequent stream water samples (AS) during storm events

steps.description: Events were considered separately if they were separated by a period of at least 24 h without rainfall.
steps.searchable_summary: Define criteria for separating distinct storm events based on a minimum period without rainfall.

steps.description: Stream water at the catchment outlet (SW) and wells (GW1 to GW4) were sampled fortnightly, as well as prior to, during, and following precipitation events.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect stream water samples at the catchment outlet and well water samples on a fortnightly basis and during precipitation events.

steps.description: Soil water was sampled fortnightly using Teflon suction lysimeters, installed at three locations: deciduous hillslope (SS1), coniferous hillslope (SS2), and riparian zone (SSr).Three soil depths for each location: 10 cm for the organic layer (Ah horizon), 20 and 60 cm for the mineral layers (B and C horizons).
steps.searchable_summary: Collect soil water samples using Teflon suction lysimeters at specified locations and depths on a fortnightly basis.
inputs: Locations (deciduous hillslope SS1, coniferous hillslope SS2, riparian zone SSr)

steps.description: Overland flow (OF) that occurred on lower hillslope was sampled using 1 and 2 m long gutters sealed to the soil surface, which diverted surface runoff to 1 or 2 L plastic, blackened (to prevent light penetration which causes diatom growth) water bottles.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect overland flow samples using sealed gutters diverting surface runoff to collection bottles.

steps.description: Gutters were regularly cleaned with Milli-Q water to avoid diatom growth on their surfaces.
steps.searchable_summary: Regularly clean overland flow gutters with Milli-Q water.

steps.description: A small set of stream water and overland flow samples was set aside for geochemical and isotopic analysis (≈ 70 mL); the rest of the sample was centrifuged (1250 rpm, 8 min) to concentrate the diatoms.
steps.searchable_summary: Split stream water and overland flow samples, setting aside a volume for geochemical and isotopic analysis.
outputs: Split samples (one portion for geochemical/isotopic, one portion for diatoms)

steps.description: Prior to analysis, samples were stored at 4 • C.
steps.searchable_summary: Store collected water samples at 4°C prior to laboratory analysis.

steps.description: All water samples were analysed ... after filtration through WHATMAN GF / C glass fibre filters (< 0.45 µm).
steps.searchable_summary: Filter water samples through WHATMAN GF/C glass fibre filters (< 0.45 µm) before analysis.
inputs: WHATMAN GF/C glass fibre filters (< 0.45 µm)

methods.description: Laboratory procedures for analyzing collected water samples for various chemical parameters (EC, ions, silica, UV absorbance) and stable isotopes (18O, 2H).

steps.description: All water samples were analysed for electrical conductivity (EC), anion and cation concentrations (Cl − , NO − 3 , SO 2− 4 , Na + , K + , Mg 2+ , Ca 2+ ), silica (SiO 2 ) and UV absorbance at 254 nm (Abs 254 nm).
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze filtered water samples for EC, Cl-, NO3-, SO4--, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, SiO2, and Abs 254 nm.

steps.description: Dissolved anions and cations were analysed by ion chromatography (Dionex HPLC)
steps.searchable_summary: Measure concentrations of dissolved anions and cations using ion chromatography.

steps.description: SiO 2 by spectrophotometry (ammonium molybdate method)
steps.searchable_summary: Measure silica concentration using spectrophotometry (ammonium molybdate method).

steps.description: UV absorbance was measured by a Beckmann Coulter spectrophotometer.
steps.searchable_summary: Measure UV absorbance at 254 nm using a spectrophotometer.

steps.description: Isotopic analyses of 18 O / 16 O and 2 H / H were conducted using a LGR Liquid-Water Iso-tope Analyser (LWIA) at the Luxembourg Institute of Science and Technology (model DLT-100, version 908-0008) (Penna et al., 2010).
steps.searchable_summary: Measure 18O/16O and 2H/H isotopic ratios in water samples using a Liquid-Water Isotope Analyser.

steps.description: The analyser was connected to a LC PAL liquid auto-injector for the automatic and simultaneous measurement of 2 H / H and 18 O / 16 O ratios in water samples.
steps.searchable_summary: Connect the isotope analyser to an auto-injector for automatic sample processing.

steps.description: Data were transformed into δ notation relative to Vienna Standard Mean Ocean Water (VSMOW) standards (δ 2 H and δ 18 O in ‰).
steps.searchable_summary: Convert raw isotopic ratios (18O/16O, 2H/H) into delta (δ) notation relative to VSMOW standards.

methods.description: Comprehensive procedures for collecting diatoms from various aquatic and terrestrial substrates, preparing samples for microscopy, and identifying/counting diatom valves for ecological analysis.

steps.description: Diatom analysis was conducted for multiple sample types: stream water, overland flow, epilithon, epipelon, and diatoms attached to different substrates outside the streambed (i.e.litter, bryophytes, vegetation and soils).
steps.searchable_summary: Define the types of samples from which diatoms will be collected and analyzed.
outputs: List of sample types for diatom analysis

steps.description: A small set of stream water and overland flow samples was set aside for geochemical and isotopic analysis (≈ 70 mL); the rest of the sample was centrifuged (1250 rpm, 8 min) to concentrate the diatoms.
steps.searchable_summary: Centrifuge the portion of stream water and overland flow samples designated for diatom analysis to concentrate diatom cells.

steps.description: In addition to high-frequency sampling during rainfall events, seasonal sampling campaigns were carried out throughout the Weierbach catchment to assess the geographic and intra-annual variability of diatom communities.The following substrates were sampled in the catchment: (i) litter, bryophytes from the two hillslope classifications (hardwood and coniferous) and surface soil samples; and (ii) litter, bryophytes, and vegetation in the riparian zone.
steps.searchable_summary: Conduct seasonal sampling campaigns across the catchment to collect diatoms from various terrestrial substrates (litter, bryophytes, vegetation, soil).

steps.description: Each sample was comprised of five sub-samples collected on a 5 m transect parallel to the stream (a subsample collected every metre).Only material from the top surface, where there was greatest incident sunlight, was collected into 1 L plastic bottles.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect five sub-samples from the top surface of substrates along a 5m transect parallel to the stream to form a composite sample.

steps.description: Sample bottles containing different substrata were filled with carbonated water (1 L), carefully shaken and left to settle overnight at 0 • C.
steps.searchable_summary: Extract diatoms from substrate samples by adding carbonated water, shaking, and allowing to settle overnight at 0°C.
outputs: Diatom-containing carbonated water (supernatant) with settled substrate

steps.description: The next day, the diatom-filled, carbonated water was recovered by passing it through a 1 mm screen.
steps.searchable_summary: Separate diatom-filled carbonated water from substrate material by passing through a 1mm screen.

steps.description: Sample substrate was then rinsed with additional carbonated water to remove as many diatoms from the sampled substrate as possible.This procedure was repeated several times until a 2 L sample volume was achieved.
steps.searchable_summary: Rinse the remaining substrate multiple times with additional carbonated water to maximize diatom recovery, achieving a target volume.

steps.description: The recovered sample, now with substrate removed, was stored at 0 • C for a minimum of 8 h to allow diatoms to settle, and the supernatant removed by aspiration.
steps.searchable_summary: Store the recovered diatom suspension at 0°C for at least 8 hours to allow diatoms to settle, then remove the supernatant by aspiration.

steps.description: During the same catchment-wide campaigns, epilithic (instream stone substrata) and epipelic (in-stream sediment or soil substrata) samples were also collected, treated and counted following European standards CEN 13946 andCEN 14407 (European Committee for Standardization, 2003, 2004).For epilithic samples a minimum of five stones from the main flow and well-lit stream reaches were brushed to collect the diatom biofilm
steps.searchable_summary: Collect epilithic diatom samples by brushing stones from stream reaches, following European standard CEN 13946.

steps.description: epipelic samples were collected by disturbing small pools with sediment bottoms and then pipetting a superficial layer of 5-10 mm of sediment from reach pools.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect epipelic diatom samples by disturbing sediment pools and pipetting the superficial layer, following European standard CEN 14407.

steps.description: All samples were preserved with 4% formaldehyde
steps.searchable_summary: Preserve all collected diatom samples using 4% formaldehyde solution.

steps.description: and treated with hot hydrogen peroxide to obtain clean frustule suspensions.After eliminating the organic matter from the diatom suspensions,
steps.searchable_summary: Clean diatom frustules by treating samples with hot hydrogen peroxide to remove organic matter.
outputs: Diatom suspensions with organic matter removed

steps.description: diluted HCl was added to remove the calcium carbonate and avoid its precipitation later, which would make diatom frustule observation difficult.
steps.searchable_summary: Add diluted HCl to oxidized samples to dissolve and remove calcium carbonate.
outputs: Diatom suspensions with calcium carbonate removed

steps.description: Finally, oxidized samples were rinsed with deionized water by decantation of the suspension several times,
steps.searchable_summary: Rinse cleaned diatom suspensions multiple times with deionized water using decantation.

steps.description: and permanent slides were mounted with Naphrax ® .
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare permanent microscopic slides of cleaned diatom frustules using Naphrax® mounting medium.

steps.description: Diatom valves were identified and counted (≈ 400 valves) on microscopic slides with a light microscope (Leica DMRX ® ).
steps.searchable_summary: Identify and count approximately 400 diatom valves on each microscopic slide using a light microscope.
outputs: Valve counts per species per sample

steps.description: For the autecological assignment of the diatom species we relied on (1) the Denys (1991) diatom ecological classification system refined by Van Dam et al. (1994), which is, as far as we know, the only formal classification of the occurrence of freshwater diatoms in relation to moisture; and (2) the associated hydrological units assigned by Pfister et al. (2009) to the five diatom occurrence classes defined by Van Dam et al. (1994).
steps.searchable_summary: Assign autecological classifications and associated hydrological units to identified diatom species based on specified classification systems.

steps.description: We express these results as relative abundance (percentage) of aerial valves, i.e. categories 4 and 5 of Van Dam's et al. (1994) classification.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate and express the relative abundance (percentage) of diatom species classified as aerial valves (categories 4 and 5 of Van Dam et al., 1994).
inputs: Van Dam et al. (1994) classification categories (4 and 5)
outputs: Relative abundance data for aerial diatoms (percentage)
